title: Needle-felted dragonfly
subtitle: A compact iridescent body and gossamer wings in merino and silk
excerpt: Build a slender dragonfly body in layers of coloured merino, then add fine silk-hanky wings using a felting needle. The finished piece can be wired to a brooch back, pinned to a wreath, or used as a cake topper.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Dragonfly forms in needle-felting are a long-established beginner project in British needle-felting guilds; the wire-and-silk-hanky wing method is standard technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. The barbs catch the fibre scales on each stab, locking them into a firm mass.
Thin sheets of degummed silk fibre stretched over a frame and dried. They separate into translucent layers that needle-felt or wet-felt onto a wool base to add sheen and colour depth.

content.text: A needle-felted dragonfly is built from a narrow rolled body, two sets of wings made from

, and optional wire for rigidity. The whole piece takes about an hour. Use any colour combination you like; dragonflies come in metallic blues, greens, and bronze tones, but there are no wrong choices here.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam pad only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

items.name: Merino roving, main colour (teal, blue, or green)
Merino roving, accent colour (gold or copper)
Fine wire, 0.5 mm gauge (florist or jewellery wire)

content.text: Pull off a length of merino about 30 cm long and roll it tightly between your palms into a sausage shape about 7 cm long and 1 cm in diameter. Place it on the foam mat and stab with the size-38 needle to compact it, working along the whole length. Rotate the body a quarter-turn every few passes to keep the shape even. Felt until the surface is firm and no loose fibres lift away.

Pull small wisps of accent colour and wrap them around the lower two-thirds of the body at regular intervals, about 1 cm apart. Needle in place with the size-40 needle, working carefully to keep the bands defined. The size-40 needle leaves a smoother finish than the 38 for this surface detail.

Roll two tiny balls of dark merino (about 4 mm diameter each) on the mat. Needle each one until firm, then position one on each side of the head end and stab to attach them. Check both eyes sit level before fully securing.

Separate a single layer from the silk hankies and lay it flat on the foam mat. Cut or tear two large oval shapes (about 8 cm by 4 cm each) for the upper wings and two smaller ovals (about 5 cm by 3 cm) for the lower wings. To stiffen the wings, fold each wire length along the centre of one wing shape and needle the silk lightly over the wire to hold it in place. The wire gives each wing its outline; the
just secures the silk to it without compressing the sheer quality.

Position the two upper wings at the top of the body, just behind the eyes, and the lower wings slightly further back. Wrap a small wisp of merino over each wing base to hold it against the body, then needle through the merino into the body to lock everything in place. Check the wings splay symmetrically before the merino sets.

items.problem: The body is lumpy rather than smoothly cylindrical
items.cause: Uneven initial rolling or uneven needle pressure
items.fix: Work more passes along the thicker sections, rotating the body frequently. A few firm strokes with the size-40 needle smooth out surface lumps without distorting the shape.

items.problem: The wing silk tears when the needle passes through it
items.cause: Too many needle stabs in the same spot, or using the size-36 needle on silk
items.fix: Use only the size-40 needle on the silk and keep each area to one or two passes. Silk hankies are fragile; a light touch is all that is needed to attach them.

items.cause: Wire too fine or not reaching close enough to the wing edge
items.fix: Cut a second piece of wire and run it around the outer edge of the drooping wing. Fold the silk edge over it and needle lightly to secure.

content.text: To make a brooch, stitch a brooch back to the underside of the body with a few passes of strong thread through the felt. The wire inside the wings holds its position and can be gently bent to adjust the angle. Store the finished piece in a box rather than a bag to protect the silk wings.
